comment.body:
Alma, I just read your other chat about the greeks and it helped clarify some stuff, especially the part about Gamma. I wanted to ask though, in regards to Delta, I always thought that if you expect a move up, it'd be better to get an option with higher Delta, but from your explanation, it kind of seems that if for example:

Stock is at 100. You expect a move to 110.

It's "better" to get an OTM 105 or even 110 instead of an ATM or ITM 100 or 95 due to how delta and premium behaves. Is this right? Is this why you tend to prefer 20-25 delta options? WOuld it be possible to get a bit more of information about this? Thanks a lot! :)
comment.raw_body:
Alma, I just read your other chat about the greeks and it helped clarify some stuff, especially the part about Gamma. I wanted to ask though, in regards to Delta, I always thought that if you expect a move up, it'd be better to get an option with higher Delta, but from your explanation, it kind of seems that if for example:

Stock is at 100. You expect a move to 110.

It's "better" to get an OTM 105 or even 110 instead of an ATM or ITM 100 or 95 due to how delta and premium behaves. Is this right? Is this why you tend to prefer 20-25 delta options? WOuld it be possible to get a bit more of information about this? Thanks a lot! :)

comment.body: What I mean by "better" due to how premium behaves was your explanation about how the first 1$ is a 100% gain, while the 2nd is a 50%. So if you're buying OTM calls, the premium you're gaining with the price increase is higher than with an ATM/ITM option. Am I understanding this correctly?

comment.body: Hi Alma, I have a question. When we talk about a crowded trade—like I.e the one with the VIX calls or puts trades during the gap down on weekend —we know it’s better to stay out because it’s “crowded.” At the same time, we also know that if a particular contract and strike are heavily traded, then the market maker (MM) will have greater risk exposure and will raise the implied volatility (IV) for that contract, so it becomes crowded as well. Based on what criteria can we determine whether it is convenient to open a position in that trade simply because the MM is pricing a higher IV? Does this signal that the probability of the market moving there increases, or is it the opposite precisely because everyone is trading that strike? I  studied all you educational post but still missing this nuance
comment.raw_body: Hi Alma, I have a question.
